title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Bowheads are extremely vocal animals and use sound to communicate and navigate in ice-choked Arctic waters. Bowheads weigh up to 80 tons -- and half that weight is an insulating layer of blubber that can be up to 20 inches thick. That blubber made these animals a prime target for commercial whalers in the 19th century. Bowheads render more oil than any other whale, and like their close cousins, the right whale, bowheads float when killed. European whalers first targeted bowhead stocks in the North Atlantic waters off Greenland in the 1500s. Detailed historical whaling accounts, and records of the quantity of bowhead whale oil marketed, offer insight to the number of whales harvested. title: Alaska fish receive clean bill of health
summary: A new state study has found low levels of mercury and other metals in fish frequently eaten in Alaska. The study, by Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation's seafood and safety laboratory, was announced Friday by Gov. Frank Murkowski. Mercury levels in wild Alaska salmon were among the lowest of the fish tested, he said. "These results reinforce what we've known all along -- that Alaska's fish are not only very tasty, healthy and full of important nutrients, but they are safe to eat, especially wild Alaska salmon," Murkowski said. The Food and Drug Administration's level of concern kicks in at one part per million of methyl mercury concentration. summary: Enough of the water escaped filters to cause Chugach's Beluga generator No. 3 to trip off, Chugach spokeswoman Patti Bogan said. That event, at 12:14 p.m., caused an imbalance in the power load, triggering other generators at the Beluga plant and the Bradley Lake Hydroelectric Project on the Kenai Peninsula to shut down. In turn, that set off more shutdowns as several other utilities shed portions of the power load to safeguard the grid, they said. Among utilities that buy power from Chugach and that had to shed power to preserve their systems were Municipal Light & Power in Anchorage, Matanuska Electric Association and Golden Valley Electric Association, which supplies electricity to a broad region from Cantwell to Delta, including Fairbanks. summary: Razaya was brought to the hospital's emergency room by her parents, Rateef Morris and Sandina Dunham, on Sept. 7 with a severe skull fracture, Bridges said. Doctors at the emergency room notified police that they had a baby with a nonaccidental, life-threatening injury, Bridges said, and detectives started an investigation. Bridges said Razaya's injuries were not consistent with falling three feet. They were more on par with going through a windshield in a car accident or being dropped from a three-story building, she said. "I'm not saying that's what happened in this case," just that the injuries were of that magnitude, she said. "We have no doubt that it's a homicide," Bridges said. summary: Auction will aid cancer victims through fly-fishing retreat The 2003 Casting for Recovery silent auction is scheduled for 5 to 8 p.m. Friday at Side Street Espresso, 412 G St. Casting for Recovery is a national program that provides physical, emotional and spiritual healing through fly-fishing retreats in a beautiful, natural setting. Auction proceeds will support a local fly-fishing retreat for women affected by breast cancer. More than 100 Alaskans have attended the free retreats, which include lodging and meals. The auction is sponsored by the YWCA of Anchorage, Side Street Espresso, Casting for Recovery, Breast Cancer Focus Inc. and Alaska Distributors Co. For more information, call Lupe Chavez, 644-9600. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The delinquency rate for Alaska Housing Finance Corp. mortgages was 3.94 percent in July, compared with 4.12 percent in June and 3.72 percent in July of last year, the state lending agency said. For mobile home loans, the July delinquency rate was 3.83 percent, down from 5.18 percent in June and 9.78 percent in July last year. During July, Alaska Housing foreclosed on four loans, compared with one each a month earlier and in July 2002. The agency held 27,550 loans in July, 9 percent fewer than a year earlier. summary: Man charged with reckless endangerment in shooting The Associated Press ANCHORAGE (September 29, 5:15 p.m. ADT) - A Wasilla man was arrested after he fired a handgun at a party that struck another man in the buttocks, Alaska State Troopers said. Jobie Karr, 40, is charged with first-degree assault, two counts of fourth-degree misconduct involving a weapon and reckless endangerment, troopers said. The victim, Jeff Navarette, 24, of Anaheim, Calif., was taken by Lifeflight helicopter to Providence Alaska Medical Center and was listed in stable condition Sunday afternoon.
